fuzzy_prd: We need a pluggable metrics collection component that acts as a central hub for gathering health and usage signals from different parts of the application. The component should support registering multiple data sources, each capable of contributing a list of named numeric measurements. During startup, the component should go through a two-stage initialization process: first verifying that all registered sources are healthy, then pulling in their measurements. If any source reports a problem during the first stage, we stop right there and report which stage failed. Same deal in the second stage. Once initialized, the component should check its own configuration for obvious problems — like missing required identifiers, measurements without names, measurements missing their sampling logic, or duplicate measurement names. The system needs clear on/off control: when turned off entirely, it should be completely inert and skip all the above checks. When running, it periodically samples every measurement on a configurable schedule. On startup it should print a human-readable message reflecting the current mode, similar to how the auth module announces its status. It should also expose a single HTTP endpoint so external monitoring tools can pull all the collected values. When the feature is disabled, that HTTP route should not be registered at all. The component must integrate cleanly with the broader application framework, following the same lifecycle conventions that other subsystems use — provision, validate, start, stop, and route registration.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default namespace is 'gotenberg'. The flag is --prometheus-namespace with default value 'gotenberg'. If no namespace is provided, 'gotenberg' is used, not empty string.
The default collect interval is 1 second (time.Duration(1)*time.Second). The flag is --prometheus-collect-interval with default value of 1s.

trigger_keywords: how to detect provider validation failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Provider validation failure is detected by checking if the error message has the prefix 'get metrics providers:'. The Provision method wraps the error as fmt.Errorf("get metrics providers: %w", err). The neutral category reported is 'provider_validation'.

trigger_keywords: how to detect metrics collection failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Metrics collection failure is detected by checking if the error message has the prefix 'get metrics:'. The Provision method wraps the error as fmt.Errorf("get metrics: %w", err). The neutral category reported is 'metrics_collection'.
The exact error message for empty namespace is 'namespace must not be empty'. The neutral category reported is 'namespace_empty'.
The exact error message for empty metric name is 'metric name cannot be empty'. The neutral category reported is 'metric_name_empty'.

The exact error message for nil read method is "metric '%s' has nil read method" (with the metric name substituted). Detection uses strings.Contains for 'nil read method'. The neutral category reported is 'metric_read_missing'.

The exact error message for duplicate metric is "metric '%s' is already registered" (with the metric name substituted). Detection uses strings.Contains for 'is already registered'. The neutral category reported is 'metric_duplicate'.

trigger_keywords: what order are validation rules checked
which validation rule is checked first
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Validation checks rules in this strict order: (1) namespace must not be empty, (2) metric name cannot be empty, (3) metric has nil read method, (4) metric name is already registered (duplicate). The first violated rule stops validation and reports that error.

trigger_keywords: what is the startup message when disabled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When collection is disabled (disableCollect=true), the startup message is exactly 'collect disabled' (no trailing newline from the method itself, but the output line ends with newline in the adapter output). When enabled it is 'collecting metrics'.

The HTTP scrape endpoint path is exactly '/prometheus/metrics'. The HTTP method is GET. When collection is disabled, Routes() returns nil (no routes), resulting in routes=0. When enabled, exactly one route is returned.

Route logging is controlled by the --prometheus-disable-route-logging flag (boolean). Default is false. When set to true, the route's DisableLogging field is true. In output this appears as 'disable_logging=true' or 'disable_logging=false'.

Collection is disabled via the --prometheus-disable-collect boolean flag. Default is false. When true, Provision exits early without consulting any provider, Validate always passes, Start is a no-op, StartupMessage returns 'collect disabled', and Routes returns nil.
Stop does nothing and always returns nil. The Stop method signature is Stop(_ context.Context) error. The output is always 'stopped=true' since no error is raised.
The Description field of a Metric is optional. Only Name (non-empty) and Read (non-nil) are required. Description is passed to the Prometheus gauge Help field and can be an empty string.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how the auth module announces its status
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The StartupMessage() method in the App interface (pkg/gotenberg/modules.go). Each module implementing the App interface provides its own StartupMessage() string method to print a custom message at startup. The Prometheus module returns 'collect disabled' or 'collecting metrics'.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same lifecycle conventions that other subsystems use — provision, validate, start, stop, and route registration
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The gotenberg module interfaces defined in pkg/gotenberg/modules.go: Module (Descriptor), Provisioner (Provision), Validator (Validate), App (Start/StartupMessage/Stop), and api.Router (Routes). The Prometheus struct implements all of these interfaces as enforced by interface guards in prometheus.go.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That specific detail is not covered by the available constraints. Please check the source code in pkg/modules/prometheus/prometheus.go for the exact implementation.

feature1_provisioning.json.description: Provision the metrics-collection component. When collection is enabled, the component gathers metrics from every registered provider component before it can run; provisioning happens in two ordered stages. First each provider is initialized and self-validated; if any provider reports itself invalid, provisioning fails at the provider-validation stage. Then each valid provider is asked for its list of metrics; if any provider fails to produce its metrics, provisioning fails at the metrics-collection stage. When all providers validate and yield their metrics, provisioning succeeds with collection enabled. When collection is disabled, provisioning succeeds immediately without consulting any provider. The result reports whether provisioning succeeded, and on success the collection mode, and on failure a neutral category naming the stage that failed.

feature2_validation.json.description: Validate the configuration of the metrics-collection component after provisioning. When collection is disabled, validation always passes regardless of any other setting. When collection is enabled, the following rules are checked in order: the metrics namespace must not be empty; every gathered metric must have a non-empty name; every gathered metric must carry a read function that supplies its current value; and metric names must be unique across all gathered metrics. The first violated rule determines the outcome. A passing configuration reports validity; a failing one reports invalidity together with a neutral category identifying which rule was broken (empty namespace, empty metric name, missing read function, or duplicate metric name).

feature3_1_startup_message.json.description: Report the startup message of the metrics-collection component. The message reflects the current collection mode: when collection is disabled the component announces that collecting is turned off, and when collection is enabled it announces that it is collecting metrics. The output is exactly the announcement text.

feature3_2_start_collection.json.description: Start the metrics-collection component after provisioning. When collection is disabled, starting is a no-op that succeeds immediately. When collection is enabled, the component begins periodically sampling each gathered metric (using the configured collection interval) and starting succeeds. The result reports whether the start succeeded and the active collection mode.

feature3_3_stop.json.description: Stop the metrics-collection component. Stopping always succeeds and requires no teardown work; the result simply reports that the component stopped.

feature4_metrics_route.json.description: Expose the HTTP route through which collected metrics are scraped. When collection is disabled, the component contributes no routes. When collection is enabled, it contributes exactly one route: an HTTP GET endpoint at the metrics path. The route also carries a per-route logging switch; enabling the route-logging-disabled setting turns logging off for that route. The result reports the number of contributed routes and, for each, its HTTP method, path, and whether logging is disabled.
